prompt:
You are the Verify phase of CREST (a 5-phase project methodology: Context, Research, Execute, Synthesize, Verify). The user has completed Execute and needs a structured verdict on whether the work satisfies the original Context requirements. Original Context: Build a simple, secure REST API in Go that accepts customer feedback via POST /feedback endpoint. The API must validate input fields (name length 2-100, email format, rating 1-5, optional comment max 1000 chars), persist to SQLite with a 30-day retention rule, return a 201 with the stored record, log all requests at INFO level with request_id correlation, expose GET /healthz returning 200 when DB is reachable, and serve on port 8080. Implementer reports: Created main.go (180 lines), feedback.go (130 lines), db.go (95 lines), with input validation, SQLite persistence via mattn/go-sqlite3, structured logging with request IDs, and healthz endpoint. The submitter's Execute phase report says: 'Wrote the four files. 405 lines total. All endpoints work. Used golang-migrate for schema. Added rate limiting (100 req/min per IP) as a bonus. Tests: 12 unit tests passing, 5 integration tests passing. Lint: clean. go vet: clean. Race detector: clean. Build: green.'

Additional Context Detail: The customer feedback system will be used in a production e-commerce environment handling approximately 500 feedback submissions per day at launch, growing to an estimated 5000 per day within six months. The system must comply with GDPR for EU customer data, requiring the 30-day retention rule to be strictly enforced via automated deletion jobs, not just application-level filtering. Email addresses must be validated to RFC 5322 format and also checked against a DNS MX record lookup to reject obviously invalid domains. The rating field must be bounded to integers 1 through 5 inclusive; any other input including 0, negative numbers, decimals, or strings must return a 400 Bad Request with a structured error response. Comments must be sanitized to prevent XSS attacks if rendered in a web dashboard. Rate limiting at 100 req/min per IP must use a sliding window algorithm, not a fixed window, to prevent burst attacks. Database migrations must be reversible. Logs must include request_id, method, path, status_code, duration_ms, client_ip, and must NOT include any PII (name, email, or full comment text).

Additional Implementer Notes: The implementer reports that rate limiting was added using a token bucket algorithm (not sliding window as requested) implemented in a custom middleware (rate_limit.go, 75 lines). The implementer chose token bucket because it was simpler than sliding window and they believed the difference was immaterial at the stated traffic scale. Email validation uses a regex pattern (RFC 5322 simplified) but no DNS MX lookup. The retention policy is implemented as a Go background goroutine that runs every 24 hours and deletes records older than 30 days, but it logs to a separate file (retention.log) rather than the main request log. XSS sanitization is implemented using the bluemonday library. The migration files are forward-only (no down migrations) due to 'time constraints' per the implementer's commit message. The unit tests cover input validation but not rate limiting, retention, or sanitization. Integration tests cover all endpoints with a real SQLite file in /tmp.

Provide a structured verdict with sections for: (1) Requirements coverage (each Context item, pass/partial/miss), (2) Concerns (anything suspicious, undocumented, or that should be re-verified), (3) Risks (security, performance, operational), (4) Verdict (GO / GO with caveats / NO-GO with reasons), (5) Required follow-ups. Be specific, cite the file/line when claiming something, and be honest if you cannot verify a claim from the evidence given.
